summary: Charity Majors responds to the backlash from her previous post about prioritizing your core job responsibilities, addressing objections about glue work, DEI labor, gendered dynamics, and management failures. She clarifies that while extracurricular work like ERGs and mentoring should factor into promotions, they cannot substitute for fulfilling the technical requirements you were hired for. She acknowledges her advice assumes a high-trust environment and encourages engineers to take ownership of career clarity even when managers fail them.
key_points: Your core job responsibilities come first, but extracurricular work like ERGs and glue work should absolutely factor into promotions when core duties are met
Promotion requires fulfilling the technical requirements of the next level — DEI work and glue work alone cannot substitute for that
Engineers should proactively seek clarity on their core responsibilities rather than waiting for good management
The advice assumes a high-trust work environment, which not everyone has the privilege of operating in
Job descriptions are living documents that should be revisited and negotiated, not assumed to be static
money_quotes: If you ARE competently performing your core responsibilities as a database engineer, and are performing them at the next level, then all your additional work for the company — on DEI, ERGs, mentoring, interviewing, etc — adds up to a MASSIVELY compelling body of work, and a powerful argument for promotion.
I am writing this post because good managers are in scarce supply, and I don't want your career to be on hold until you happen to get a good one.
You shouldn't HAVE to do their job for them. But why let a shitty manager hurt your career any more than you must?
Communication and persuasion are what upper levels are all about.
A successful career in sociotechnical systems means leveling up your social repertoire as well as your technical skills.
key_insight: Fulfilling your core job responsibilities is the foundation that makes all your other valuable contributions — glue work, DEI labor, mentoring — count toward career advancement rather than being seen as distractions from unfinished work.
